incorporations.reason: The inline gloss '[vessel, tub]' broke the narrative flow; the italicised foreign word is sufficient, as the meaning is clear from context

incorporations.original: spread itself out on either bank
incorporations.replacement: spread itself out upon either bank
incorporations.reason: 'Upon' better matches the slightly elevated register used throughout the translation and echoes the Italian 'sull'una e sull'altra riva'

incorporations.original: drew the outlines of their yards against the sky
incorporations.replacement: drew against the sky the spars of their yards
incorporations.reason: Italian 'le aste dei loro pennoni' literally means the shafts/spars of their yards; 'outlines' (Claude) was vague, 'shafts' (Gemini) was closer but 'spars' is the precise English nautical term for 'aste'
The standard English idiom is 'once and for all'; Claude's 'for once and for all' is a minor awkwardness

incorporations.original: the only one dressed in plain clothes
incorporations.replacement: the only one dressed in civilian clothes
incorporations.reason: 'Civilian clothes' is the standard English rendering of Italian 'vestito in borghese' and makes the military-versus-civilian contrast explicit, which is the author's point
Italian 'a ritroso della loro volontà' literally means 'against their will'; 'wishes' is closer to 'volontà' than 'inclinations' while remaining less blunt than 'will'
'Garb' better suits the slightly sardonic tone of the passage and the Italian 'abito da forzato', which denotes a distinctive uniform rather than ordinary clothing

incorporations.original: in a deep silence, to the amusement of the policemen
incorporations.replacement: in deep silence, amid the jests of the policemen
incorporations.reason: Italian 'tra le facezie dei poliziotti' means 'amid the jests/jokes'; Claude's 'to the amusement' inverts who is acting — the policemen are actively jesting, not merely amused
Italian 'un figlio' specifically means 'a son'; 'child' loses the gender, and the narrative context (the firstborn son) confirms the specificity
Italian 'disilluso' maps directly to 'disillusioned'; 'disenchanted' carries unwanted connotations of fairy-tale magic

edgren_influences.note: Edgren confirms 'aringa' is literally 'herring' (not a general term for small fish), validating the direct translation 'the little herring moved to meet the shark' rather than a looser paraphrase

edgren_influences.edgren_definition: scafo [? Gr. -phos, basin], M.: hulk (carcass of a ship)
edgren_influences.note: Edgren gives 'hulk' as the primary sense, but in modern English 'hulk' implies a derelict vessel; 'hull' was preferred as the standard term for a ship's body, the meaning clearly intended by the context of a cutter appearing on the horizon

edgren_influences.edgren_definition: ammonimento, M.: admonishment. -nîre [L. monere, teach], TR.: admonish; warn
edgren_influences.note: Edgren gives both 'admonishment' and 'warn'; 'warning' was chosen over 'admonishment' as more natural for a cannon-shot — the shot warns rather than admonishes
